assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements of a non-investment entity.
The intragroup dividend has been declared and recognised as an intercompany receivable/payable, so it is analysed as a recognised intragroup balance rather than a forecast transaction.
The analysis is limited to hedge accounting eligibility for foreign currency risk under the cited IFRS guidance.

approaches.label: General intragroup prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally be with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup items are ordinarily not eligible there.
approaches.practical_implication: A booked intragroup dividend receivable cannot be hedged in consolidation unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party exposures and says intragroup hedge accounting is generally only in individual or separate financial statements.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 provides a specific consolidation exception for the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend balance is a recognised intragroup monetary receivable/payable.
The entities involved have different functional currencies so FX differences are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Only the foreign currency risk is designated as the hedged risk.
The IFRS 9 designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met at inception.
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge may be documented in consolidation, but only for the residual consolidated FX exposure on the intercompany dividend balance.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria for any qualifying hedging relationship.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction exception
approaches.reasoning: A separate exception exists for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions in foreign currency that will affect consolidated profit or loss. That does not fit a dividend already recognised as receivable.
conditions: The item would need to be a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction.
It would need to be denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it.
Its foreign currency risk would need to affect consolidated profit or loss.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on the forecast-transaction exception once the dividend is already booked as an intercompany receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits foreign currency risk on a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to qualify in consolidation under specified conditions.
Paragraph B6.3.5 says forecast intragroup transactions cannot qualify unless the foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: Net investment hedge accounting is a separate model for foreign currency risk on net assets of a foreign operation. It does not treat an intragroup dividend receivable itself as the hedged item.
conditions: The hedged item would need to be an amount of net assets of a foreign operation included in the consolidated financial statements.
The designated risk would be the foreign exchange exposure between the functional currency of the foreign operation and the relevant parent entity.
approaches.practical_implication: Use this model only if the risk management objective is to hedge the foreign operation net investment, not the dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct type of hedging relationship.
Paragraphs 10–14 and related guidance define the hedged item in a net investment hedge as net assets of the foreign operation and set the foreign currency risk framework for that model.
